name: Celery
what_it_does: Distributed task queue for Python that enables asynchronous, scheduled, and periodic task execution via message brokers (Redis, RabbitMQ). Flower provides a web UI and limited REST API for monitoring.
use_cases: Offloading slow or compute-intensive work from agents to background workers
Scheduling periodic tasks (cron-style) in Python applications
Distributing AI inference or data processing workloads across multiple workers
Retry logic for unreliable external API calls with configurable backoff
Fan-out workflows where an agent triggers parallel task execution
not_for: Long-running durable workflows requiring persistent state across failures (use Temporal instead)
Teams without Redis or RabbitMQ infrastructure to manage
Real-time sub-second task execution with strict latency requirements
best_when: A Python application needs to offload background work to distributed workers with retry logic and scheduling, and your team is comfortable managing a message broker.
avoid_when: You need durable workflow orchestration with human-in-the-loop steps, non-Python workers, or a managed service without infrastructure overhead.

auth.notes: No built-in auth API. Authentication is delegated to the message broker (Redis AUTH, RabbitMQ credentials) and the transport layer. Flower monitoring UI has basic HTTP auth. No API key or token system.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Fully free and open source (BSD license). Infrastructure costs (Redis, RabbitMQ, workers) are separate.
pricing.paid_tiers: No paid tiers. Commercial support available from third parties.
pricing.notes: Celery itself is free. The cost is the infrastructure: Redis or RabbitMQ broker, and compute for worker processes. Managed Redis/RabbitMQ from cloud providers is the typical operational cost.

performance.rate_limits: No inherent rate limits; throughput depends on broker and worker capacity

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Task exceptions are captured and stored in the result backend with full Python tracebacks. Worker-level errors (broker disconnect, serialization) surface as logs rather than structured errors. Flower REST API returns basic task status without rich error context.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Tasks can be made idempotent by setting task_acks_late=True and using custom task IDs. No built-in idempotency key mechanism — developers must implement deduplication. Chord/group workflows can produce duplicate results on retry if not carefully designed.
known_agent_gotchas: No built-in REST API — agents cannot dispatch or query tasks without embedding Celery as a Python dependency or using Flower's limited API
Result backend must be configured separately (Redis, database) — without it task results are lost
Serialization format defaults to JSON but complex Python objects require pickle — pickle introduces security risks
Task visibility timeout must be tuned or long-running tasks get re-queued (ghost tasks)
Celery 5.x broke backward compatibility — pin version carefully in production
Worker concurrency model (prefork vs gevent vs eventlet) must match workload — wrong choice causes subtle bugs
Periodic tasks (beat scheduler) require a separate beat process — forgetting to run it silently breaks cron jobs
No native support for task dependencies with complex DAG structures beyond basic chains and chords

score_rationale: Celery is the battle-tested Python task queue with a large ecosystem and proven reliability for background job processing. However, its agent-friendliness is severely limited by the absence of a REST API (agents must be Python), no built-in idempotency, opaque worker-level error handling, and significant operational complexity (broker management, beat scheduler, result backend). Suitable for agents embedded in Python applications but poor for cross-language or externally-orchestrated agent systems.
